documents.content:
Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is a hormonal disorder common among women of reproductive age.
        Women with PCOS may have infrequent or prolonged menstrual periods or excess male hormone (androgen) levels.
        The ovaries may develop numerous small collections of fluid (follicles) and fail to regularly release eggs.
        PCOS is one of the most common endocrine disorders, affecting 6-12% of women of reproductive age.
        It is a leading cause of infertility and is associated with long-term health risks including type 2 diabetes and heart disease.

Common PCOS symptoms include:
        - Irregular periods: Infrequent, irregular or prolonged menstrual cycles are the most common sign.
        - Excess androgen: Elevated levels of male hormones may result in physical signs such as excess facial and body hair (hirsutism),
          and occasionally severe acne and male-pattern baldness.
        - Polycystic ovaries: Ovaries might be enlarged and contain follicles surrounding the eggs.
        - Weight gain: Many women with PCOS are overweight or obese.
        - Skin changes: Darkening of skin (acanthosis nigricans), skin tags.
        - Thinning hair or hair loss on the scalp.
        - Fatigue and low energy.
        - Mood changes including depression and anxiety.
        - Pelvic pain.
        - Headaches.
        Symptoms typically begin around the time of the first menstrual period during puberty.

The exact cause of PCOS is unknown. Factors that might play a role include:
        - Insulin resistance: Up to 70% of women with PCOS have insulin resistance. When cells can't use insulin properly,
          the pancreas secretes more insulin, which may cause the ovaries to produce more androgens.
        - Low-grade inflammation: Research has shown that women with PCOS have a type of low-grade inflammation
          that stimulates polycystic ovaries to produce androgens.
        - Heredity: PCOS tends to run in families. If your mother or sister has PCOS, your risk is higher.
        - Excess androgen: The ovaries produce abnormally high levels of androgen, resulting in hirsutism and acne.
        - Genetics: Multiple genes may be associated with PCOS.

PCOS is diagnosed when at least two of three criteria are met (Rotterdam criteria):
        1. Irregular periods or no periods, caused by infrequent ovulation or failure to ovulate.
        2. High androgen levels — either a blood test showing high testosterone, or physical signs (excess body/facial hair, acne, or male-pattern baldness).
        3. Polycystic ovaries — where the ovaries are enlarged and contain many fluid-filled sacs (follicles) which surround the eggs.

        Diagnostic process includes:
        - Physical exam and medical history.
        - Blood tests: hormone levels (LH, FSH, testosterone, DHEA-S), glucose tolerance test, lipid profile.
        - Pelvic ultrasound to check ovary appearance and uterine lining.
        - Ruling out other conditions: thyroid dysfunction, adrenal hyperplasia, hyperprolactinemia.

PCOS treatment focuses on managing individual concerns:
        
        Lifestyle changes (first-line treatment):
        - Weight loss (even 5-10% can improve symptoms significantly).
        - Regular exercise: at least 150 minutes moderate activity per week.
        - Low glycemic index (low-GI) diet.

        Medications for menstrual regulation:
        - Combination birth control pills (estrogen + progestin) to regulate periods and reduce androgen.
        - Progestin therapy.
        - Hormonal IUD.

        Medications for ovulation and fertility:
        - Letrozole (first-line for ovulation induction).
        - Clomiphene citrate (Clomid).
        - Metformin (improves insulin sensitivity and can restore ovulation).
        - Gonadotropins (injectable hormones).
        - In vitro fertilization (IVF) if other treatments fail.

        Medications for excess hair/acne:
        - Spironolactone (anti-androgen).
        - Eflornithine cream for facial hair.
        - Topical treatments for acne.

        Other procedures:
        - Laparoscopic ovarian drilling (LOD): surgical procedure to stimulate ovulation.

Diet plays a significant role in managing PCOS:

        Recommended foods:
        - High-fiber foods: broccoli, cauliflower, spinach, greens, beans, lentils.
        - Lean protein: chicken, fish, tofu, legumes.
        - Anti-inflammatory foods: olive oil, tomatoes, fatty fish, leafy greens, walnuts.
        - Low-GI carbs: whole grains, oats, quinoa, sweet potatoes.
        - Berries, avocados, nuts, and seeds.

        Foods to avoid or limit:
        - Refined carbohydrates: white bread, white rice, pastries.
        - Sugary foods and beverages.
        - Processed and fried foods.
        - Red meat and processed meats.
        - Dairy (some women find it worsens symptoms).

        Beneficial dietary patterns:
        - Low glycemic index (GI) diet.
        - Mediterranean diet.
        - DASH diet.
        - Anti-inflammatory diet.

        Supplements that may help: inositol (myo-inositol), vitamin D, omega-3 fatty acids, magnesium, chromium.

PCOS is one of the most common causes of female infertility, but most women with PCOS can get pregnant with treatment.

        How PCOS affects fertility:
        - Irregular or absent ovulation is the main cause of infertility.
        - Hormonal imbalances disrupt the normal ovulation cycle.
        - Insulin resistance can further suppress ovulation.

        Fertility treatment options:
        - Lifestyle modification and weight loss (can restore ovulation naturally).
        - Letrozole: preferred first-line medication for ovulation induction.
        - Clomiphene citrate (Clomid): commonly used ovulation induction drug.
        - Metformin: especially useful in insulin-resistant women.
        - Injectable gonadotropins: used when oral medications fail.
        - IUI (intrauterine insemination).
        - IVF (in vitro fertilization): highest success rates for PCOS-related infertility.

        Women with PCOS who do get pregnant should be monitored for gestational diabetes and preeclampsia.
        PCOS does not cause menopause earlier, but symptoms may improve post-menopause.

PCOS significantly impacts mental health and quality of life:

        Common mental health concerns:
        - Depression: Women with PCOS are 3x more likely to experience depression.
        - Anxiety: Higher rates of generalized anxiety disorder.
        - Body image issues due to weight gain, hirsutism, acne, and hair loss.
        - Low self-esteem and reduced quality of life.
        - Eating disorders: higher risk due to concerns about weight.
        - Sleep disorders including sleep apnea.

        Management approaches:
        - Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) is effective for depression and anxiety.
        - Mindfulness and stress reduction practices.
        - Support groups and community.
        - Treating the physical symptoms often improves mental health.
        - Antidepressants or anti-anxiety medications when appropriate.
        - Regular physical exercise (boosts mood and reduces anxiety).

        It's important for healthcare providers to screen women with PCOS for mental health conditions.

Women with PCOS face several long-term health risks:

        Metabolic risks:
        - Type 2 diabetes: Up to 50% of women with PCOS develop type 2 diabetes by age 40.
        - Insulin resistance (affects ~70% of women with PCOS).
        - Metabolic syndrome (combination of high blood pressure, high blood sugar, abnormal cholesterol).

        Cardiovascular risks:
        - Higher risk of heart disease and stroke.
        - Dyslipidemia (abnormal cholesterol and triglyceride levels).
        - Hypertension (high blood pressure).

        Reproductive and gynecological risks:
        - Endometrial cancer: due to irregular periods causing endometrial buildup.
        - Pregnancy complications: gestational diabetes, preeclampsia, premature birth.
        - Miscarriage risk may be slightly elevated.

        Other risks:
        - Sleep apnea (especially in overweight women).
        - Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD).
        - Endometrial hyperplasia.

        Regular monitoring (annual check-ups, blood sugar, blood pressure, lipid panels) is recommended.

Exercise and lifestyle changes are foundational in managing PCOS:

        Exercise benefits for PCOS:
        - Improves insulin sensitivity.
        - Helps with weight management.
        - Reduces androgen levels.
        - Improves menstrual regularity.
        - Boosts mood and reduces anxiety/depression.
        - Lowers cardiovascular risk.

        Recommended exercise types:
        - Cardio: walking, cycling, swimming, dancing (150 min/week moderate intensity).
        - Strength training: resistance exercises 2-3x per week (improves insulin sensitivity).
        - HIIT (High-Intensity Interval Training): effective for insulin resistance.
        - Yoga: reduces cortisol and androgen levels, improves mental health.
        - Pilates: core strengthening, low impact.

        Lifestyle recommendations:
        - Maintain a healthy weight (even 5% loss improves symptoms).
        - Prioritize sleep (7-9 hours per night).
        - Manage stress (chronic stress elevates cortisol and can worsen PCOS).
        - Limit alcohol.
        - Quit smoking (smoking raises androgen levels).
        - Regular medical check-ups.

PCOS can affect teenagers and adolescents, often beginning around puberty.

        Challenges in diagnosing PCOS in teens:
        - Irregular periods are normal in the first 1-2 years after menstruation begins.
        - Polycystic-appearing ovaries are common in adolescence.
        - Acne is common in all teens.

        Modified diagnostic criteria for adolescents:
        - Both irregular periods AND elevated androgen levels should be present.
        - Ultrasound is not recommended as the sole criterion in adolescents.

        Signs to watch for in teens:
        - Periods more than 90 days apart after the first 2 years of menstruation.
        - Severe acne unresponsive to standard treatments.
        - Excessive facial or body hair.
        - Unexplained weight gain, especially around the abdomen.

        Treatment in teens focuses on:
        - Lifestyle changes (diet, exercise).
        - Birth control pills to regulate periods and reduce androgen.
        - Treating acne and hirsutism.
        - Mental health support.

Common medications and supplements used for PCOS:

        Hormonal medications:
        - Combined oral contraceptives (COCs): regulate periods, reduce androgens, treat acne and hirsutism.
        - Progestins: regulate menstrual cycle.
        - Spironolactone: blocks androgens, helps with hair loss and acne.

        Insulin-sensitizing agents:
        - Metformin: reduces insulin resistance, promotes weight loss, may restore ovulation.
        - Inositol (myo-inositol and D-chiro-inositol): natural supplement, improves insulin sensitivity.

        Ovulation induction:
        - Letrozole (Femara): first-line for fertility in PCOS.
        - Clomiphene citrate (Clomid): alternative ovulation inducer.

        Supplements with evidence:
        - Myo-inositol: improves insulin sensitivity and ovarian function.
        - Vitamin D: deficiency is common in PCOS and linked to worsened symptoms.
        - Omega-3 fatty acids: reduce inflammation and androgens.
        - Magnesium: may improve insulin resistance.
        - Zinc: may help with hirsutism and acne.
        - N-acetyl cysteine (NAC): antioxidant, may improve insulin sensitivity.
        - Berberine: natural compound with similar effects to metformin.

        Always consult a healthcare provider before starting any medications or supplements.

When cooking with a PCOS dietary lens, lean protein, anti-inflammatory, and low-GI foods are recommended for PCOS management.

        Recipe 1: Lemon-Herb Chicken
        Given that lean protein is recommended for PCOS management, chicken is an excellent choice. 
        Tips for cooking chicken:
        - Grill or bake to minimize added oils and calories.
        - Use herbs and spices like thyme, rosemary, or oregano to add flavor.
        - Choose lean cuts like boneless, skinless breasts or thighs.
        - Marinate wisely with olive oil-based marinades.

        Ingredients:
        - 1 lb boneless, skinless chicken breast
        - 2 tbsp olive oil
        - 2 cloves garlic, minced
        - 1 tsp dried thyme
        - 1 tsp lemon zest
        - Salt and pepper to taste

        Instructions:
        1. Preheat your oven to 375°F (190°C) or your grill.
        2. In a small bowl, mix together olive oil, garlic, thyme, and lemon zest.
        3. Place the chicken breast in a baking dish and brush the marinade evenly over both sides.
        4. Season with salt and pepper to taste.
        5. Bake for 25-30 minutes (or grill for 5-7 minutes per side) until cooked through.

        Recipe 2: Quinoa and Roasted Vegetable Salad
        Quinoa is a low-GI complex carb that helps balance blood sugar for PCOS.

        Ingredients:
        - 1 cup quinoa, rinsed
        - 2 cups chopped vegetables (bell peppers, zucchini, broccoli)
        - 1 tbsp olive oil
        - Lemon juice and herbs

        Instructions:
        1. Cook quinoa according to package instructions.
        2. Toss veggies in olive oil and roast at 400°F (200°C) for 20 minutes.
        3. Combine quinoa and veggies, dress with lemon juice.
